headline: GRAINS CLOSE MAY AT LOWS, JUNE PLANTING WINDOW NARROWS
subheadline: Corn, beans end month down 2% as prevent plant deadline approaches June 5th.
lead: May's done and grains closed at the month's lows. Corn at $4.47 finished down 2%, beans at $11.87 gave back 1%. The selloff heading into Memorial Day cleared weak hands but left the real question: can the Belt get beans in the ground before the prevent plant deadline hits June 5th? Drought monitor shows stress building across Nebraska wheat country, and the planting window is tighter than the funds want to price.
the_takeaway: June planting pace decides whether May's lows hold or break lower.
teaser: May ends ugly for grains as prevent plant deadline looms large.

one_number.context: The insurance cutoff is five days away and Nebraska drought plus repeated freeze damage already has wheat tours adjusting schedules. If wet weather delays soybean planting past the deadline, prevented acres shift to corn or sit idle, completely reshuffling summer supply math.

sections.title: Grains End May Weak
sections.body: Corn closed Friday at **$4.47**, down 9 cents in the session and **2% for the week**. December corn at $4.75 held better but still shed 7½ cents. The selloff looked like **profit-taking ahead of Memorial Day** combined with technical selling as July corn tested the 200-day moving average. China stayed away from U.S. soybeans again Friday morning despite unknown destinations buying, keeping the demand story thin. The prevent plant deadline looms June 5th, but the market isn't pricing weather premium yet. Range-bound into summer unless the Belt can't get beans in the ground.
sections.catalyst: Profit-taking ahead of Memorial Day plus technical selling as corn tested 200-day
sections.bottom_line: May lows set the summer range until planting pace forces a break.

sections.title: Livestock Diverges on Heat
sections.body: Live cattle closed **$239.05**, down nearly 1% as cash business stayed light through Friday afternoon. Feeders fell harder to $348.42, **down 1.3%** as heat stress concerns build heading into summer. Lean hogs got hit worst, down **2.5% to $99.50** as processing margins tightened. The divergence tells the story: cattle holding value on tight supplies while hogs face seasonal pressure. Heat stress preparation becomes critical as uncomfortable cattle eat less and produce less milk and beef. The cash cattle trade hasn't reset since the last Cattle on Feed report, keeping the futures range-bound until it does.
sections.catalyst: Heat stress concerns plus light cash cattle business through Friday
sections.bottom_line: Cattle holding, hogs breaking, heat stress the summer wildcard.

sections.title: Energy Holds Iran Ease
sections.body: WTI crude held **$87.36**, down less than 1% as Iran tensions continued to ease from February's Hormuz disruptions. Natural gas at $3.29 barely moved, still **85% below its 52-week high** as storage builds for summer cooling demand. The energy complex is consolidating after the sharp moves earlier this month. Kansas Fed President Schmid warned the oil price shock might not be transitory given elevated baseline inflation, but the market isn't pricing that risk yet. Fertilizer prices remain a producer concern with the **FTC launching an antitrust investigation** into rising costs. Input costs still elevated but energy's holding pattern helps.
sections.catalyst: Continued easing of Iran-Hormuz tensions from February disruptions
sections.bottom_line: Energy consolidating, but input costs still pressuring farm margins.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC probes fertilizer pricing in antitrust investigation
outside_the_pit.body: The Federal Trade Commission confirmed it's investigating rising fertilizer costs, with Iowa farmer Lance Lillibridge telling the chairman that current prices are unsustainable and farmers are 'basically going broke.' The probe targets potential anti-competitive practices in an industry where input costs have squeezed margins across the Belt.

outside_the_pit.title: Theileria tick-borne parasite detected in Nebraska cattle
outside_the_pit.body: The Asian Longhorned Tick is spreading Theileria parasites across several Nebraska counties, adding disease pressure to drought-stressed herds. The timing hits as producers prepare for heat stress season, creating a dual management challenge that could impact summer cattle performance.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA specialty crop assistance opens June 1st enrollment
outside_the_pit.body: $1.625 billion in payments will go to eligible specialty crop producers starting next week. The program provides relief for operations hit by weather and market disruptions, though most Belt producers will be watching the broader grain programs more closely.

the_more_you_know.title: The prevent plant paradox: why June 5th matters more than price
the_more_you_know.body: June 5th is the soybean prevent plant deadline across most of the Belt, and it's become the market's invisible hand. When producers can't plant by the deadline, prevented acres either shift to corn (if its deadline hasn't passed) or sit idle collecting insurance. That acreage shuffle completely changes summer supply math regardless of what futures prices say today. The 2019 example is instructive: prevent plant hit 20 million acres, mostly corn and beans, sending prices into a completely different orbit by July. This year's tight planting window makes the deadline more binary than usual.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress; soybean planting above 85% clears prevent plant concerns
Six-day weather outlook; dry conditions through Friday keep planting window open
Weekly export sales; China soybean purchases above 400K MT would signal demand return
June planting pace versus prevent plant deadline determines summer direction

daily_quote.text: We learn from our gardens to deal with the most urgent question of the time: How much is enough?
